skill.name: improve-codebase-architecture
skill.description: Find deepening opportunities in a codebase, informed by the domain language in CONTEXT.md and the decisions in docs/adr/. Use when the user wants to improve architecture, find refactoring opportunities, consolidate tightly-coupled modules, or make a codebase more testable and AI-navigable.
skill.summary: Find deepening opportunities by analyzing module depth, interfaces, and seams to improve testability and AI-navigability.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: All 41 static findings are false positives. The scanner incorrectly flagged 'interface' as weak cryptography and backticks as shell execution. This is a documentation skill that guides AI code analysis using software architecture vocabulary (module, interface, seam, adapter). No malicious patterns, no command injection vectors, no credential access.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentified 'Interface' as Cryptographic Weakness
high_findings.description: 23 high-severity findings flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' but all occurrences are legitimate uses of the word 'interface' in software architecture context (module interfaces, not cryptographic interfaces). No cryptographic code exists.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct verification of source files confirms all flagged terms are standard software architecture vocabulary, not cryptographic algorithm references.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentified Markdown Backticks as Shell Execution
high_findings.description: 14 medium-severity findings flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' but backticks are Markdown formatting for file paths like `CONTEXT.md` and `docs/adr/`. No shell commands are executed.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backticks in source files are Markdown code span syntax for file path references, not code execution patterns.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentified Relative Path Notation as Path Traversal
high_findings.description: 4 high-severity findings flagged 'path traversal sequence' for relative paths like `../domain-model/CONTEXT-FORMAT.md`. These are valid links to sibling documentation in the skill directory structure.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The `..` in paths refers to sibling skill directories in the mattpocock/skills repository, not user file system traversal attempts.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Flagged Codebase Exploration as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: 2 low-severity findings flagged 'system reconnaissance' for using `subagent_type=Explore` to walk the codebase. This is standard Claude Code functionality for code analysis, not malicious reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Explore subagent is used to organically discover architectural friction points - a legitimate code analysis technique, not system enumeration.

content.value_statement: Codebases accumulate shallow modules that are hard to test and navigate. This skill finds deepening opportunities by analyzing module depth and seams, then guides you through refactoring decisions to make your architecture more maintainable.
actual_capabilities: Analyze module depth by comparing interface complexity to implementation complexity
Identify shallow modules with high interface-to-implementation ratio
Propose deepening opportunities using consistent architecture vocabulary
Guide refactoring decisions through a grilling conversation process
Design alternative module interfaces in parallel using sub-agents
Update project documentation (CONTEXT.md, ADRs) as design decisions crystallize
limitations: Does not execute code or run tests - only provides analysis and guidance
Requires existing CONTEXT.md and ADR files to understand domain language
Cannot automatically refactor code - guides human decision-making only
Effectiveness depends on quality of existing codebase documentation

use_cases.title: Before a major refactor
use_cases.description: Identify which modules should be deepened before tackling a large feature. Get a ranked list of architectural opportunities with trade-off analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads and senior developers planning significant code changes

use_cases.title: Improving test coverage
use_cases.description: Find modules that are hard to test through their current interface. Get recommendations for deepening that makes testing through the seam possible.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers trying to improve testability

use_cases.title: Onboarding a new team
use_cases.description: Surface the architectural shape of the codebase for new team members. Use the deepening conversation to explain why the architecture is shaped the way it is.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering managers and team leads orienting new hires

prompt_templates.title: Basic architecture review
prompt_templates.prompt: Find deepening opportunities in our codebase. Start by reading CONTEXT.md and any ADRs in docs/adr/.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want a quick overview of architectural improvement potential

prompt_templates.title: Focus on specific area
prompt_templates.prompt: Focus on modules related to [feature area]. Look for shallow modules, tight coupling across seams, and areas where understanding one concept requires bouncing between many modules.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want to focus on a specific part of the codebase

prompt_templates.title: Testability improvement
prompt_templates.prompt: Find modules that are hard to test. For each one, explain why the current interface makes testing difficult and how deepening it would help.
prompt_templates.scenario: When your goal is specifically to improve test coverage

prompt_templates.title: Interface exploration
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to explore alternative interfaces for [module name]. Help me understand the problem space first, then design 3 different interface options.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want to redesign a specific module's interface

output_examples.input: Find deepening opportunities in our payment processing code
output_examples.output:
Here are 3 deepening candidates:

1. **Order Intake Module**
   - Files: OrderHandler.ts, OrderValidator.ts, OrderPersistence.ts
   - Problem: 3 modules all doing pieces of Order intake with ordering constraints spread across them
   - Solution: Merge into one module with a clear interface for Order intake
   - Benefits: Locality for Order validation rules; Leverage via single interface for tests

2. **Payment Gateway Adapter**
   - Files: StripeClient.ts, PaymentController.ts
   - Problem: Payment logic mixed with transport concerns
   - Solution: Define a port, keep logic in deep module, inject Stripe as adapter
   - Benefits: Leverage via mock adapter for tests; Locality for payment rules

Which would you like to explore first?

output_examples.input: Design alternative interfaces for our cache module
output_examples.output:
**Problem Space:** The cache module currently couples cache invalidation to retrieval. Any client must know when to invalidate.

**Design 1 (Minimized):** 1 entry point `get(key, loader)` - loader lazy-loads on cache miss, invalidation automatic.

**Design 2 (Flexible):** Separate `get`, `set`, `invalidate` with composable invalidation policies.

**Design 3 (Common-case-first):** Async `get` returns immediately from cache, loader runs in background.

**Recommendation:** Design 1 offers highest leverage per entry point. The loader pattern eliminates invalidation responsibility from callers.

best_practices: Read CONTEXT.md and ADRs before exploring the codebase - domain language gives names to good seams
Apply the deletion test: would deleting a module concentrate complexity or move it elsewhere?
Use consistent vocabulary: say 'seam' not 'boundary', 'adapter' not 'service'
anti_patterns: Don't propose interfaces without asking the user which candidates to explore first
Don't suggest deepening a module unless it has at least two adapters (a real seam, not just indirection)
Don't surface candidates that contradict an ADR unless the friction is real enough to reopen it

faq.question: What is 'deepening' a module?
faq.answer: Deepening means increasing the leverage at a module's interface. A deep module has a lot of behavior behind a small interface. Shallow modules have interfaces nearly as complex as their implementations.

faq.question: What is a 'seam'?
faq.answer: A seam is where a module's interface lives - a place where behavior can be altered without editing in place. Seams enable testing and allow different adapters at development, test, and production time.

faq.question: Does this skill write code?
faq.answer: No. This skill analyzes code and guides refactoring decisions through conversation. It updates documentation files like CONTEXT.md but does not generate implementation code.

faq.question: What does this skill need to work well?
faq.answer: Existing CONTEXT.md and ADR files help the skill understand your domain language and existing decisions. Without these, it provides generic architecture advice rather than project-specific recommendations.

faq.question: How is this different from a linter or static analyzer?
faq.answer: Linters find code-level issues. This skill finds architectural friction points - places where understanding or changing code is harder than it should be. It focuses on module boundaries, interface design, and test surface area.

faq.question: Can this work with any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes. The vocabulary (module, interface, seam, adapter) is language-agnostic. The skill explores the codebase organically to find architectural patterns regardless of implementation language.
